front: Philosopher: cited coffeehouses, salons, Masonic lodges, and literary societies as sites for the emergence of a bourgeois public sphere in 18th-century Europe
work: The Structural Transformation of the Public Sphere

front: Work: traces the replacement of representational culture (Offentlichkeit) with rational-critical debate, then its decline through mass media and consumer culture
back: The Structural Transformation of the Public Sphere (Jürgen Habermas)
work: The Structural Transformation of the Public Sphere

front: Philosopher: Nancy Fraser proposed subaltern 'counterdiscourses' in an article 'Rethinking' this thinker's concept of the public sphere
work: The Structural Transformation of the Public Sphere

front: Concept: scenarios in which discourse is governed by basic implied rules agreed upon by all parties, enabling rationality to arise through communication
work: Communicative Rationality and the Ideal Speech Situation (Theory of Communicative Action)

front: Philosopher: divides rationality into aesthetic, normative, and cognitive-instrumental forms, expanding Max Weber's conception of rationalization
work: Communicative Rationality and the Ideal Speech Situation (Theory of Communicative Action)

front: Work: divided into volumes 'Reason and the Rationalization of Society' and 'A Critique of Functionalist Reason'; develops a theory of communicative rationality
back: The Theory of Communicative Action (Jürgen Habermas)
work: Communicative Rationality and the Ideal Speech Situation (Theory of Communicative Action)

front: Philosopher: developed discourse ethics and communicative rationality with Karl-Otto Apel through the 'Universal Pragmatics' program
work: Communicative Rationality and the Ideal Speech Situation (Theory of Communicative Action)

front: Concept: the realm of consensus-based, meaningful, symbolically mediated interaction, borrowed from Edmund Husserl's phenomenology, that is 'colonized' by markets and bureaucracy
work: System and Lifeworld (Colonization Thesis)

front: Philosopher: synthesized Parsons/Luhmann systems theory with Husserl/Schutz's phenomenological concept of the lifeworld, dividing Parsons's AGIL paradigm between them
work: System and Lifeworld (Colonization Thesis)

front: Philosopher: argued that weakness of the I and L in Parsons's AGIL paradigm can diminish legitimacy through 'colonization'
work: System and Lifeworld (Colonization Thesis)

front: Philosopher: his disciple Axel Honneth used the term 'social pathology' for distortions taking society further from ideal rationality
work: System and Lifeworld (Colonization Thesis)

front: Work: identifies three knowledge interests — understanding (humanities), criticizing oppression (social sciences), understanding nature (natural sciences) — and attempts to construct the prehistory of modern positivism
work: Knowledge and Human Interests

front: Work: begins with Hegel and Marx's 'Crisis of the Critique of Knowledge' and ends with critiques of Freud and Nietzsche
work: Knowledge and Human Interests

front: Work: a dialogue with Pope Benedict XVI (Joseph Ratzinger) about religion's role in public life and the question of terrorism
work: The Dialectics of Secularization and Post-Secularism

front: Philosopher: popularized the term 'postsecularism' and argued for 'methodological atheism' accommodating religious concerns in public discourse
work: The Dialectics of Secularization and Post-Secularism

front: Philosopher: attacked Heidegger's 'world disclosure' as devaluing reason and criticized postmodernism for 'Dionysian messianism' and 'performative contradictions' in The Philosophical Discourse of Modernity
work: The Philosophical Discourse of Modernity and Debates

front: Philosopher: called Foucault a 'crypto-normativist' who relied on Enlightenment principles while trying to refute them
work: The Philosophical Discourse of Modernity and Debates

front: Philosopher: debated Jacques Derrida; their exchanges are recorded in Philosophy in the Time of Terror
work: The Philosophical Discourse of Modernity and Debates

front: Philosopher: during the Historikerstreit, attacked apologetic tendencies in German historiography in Die Zeit, singling out Nolte and Hillgruber for downplaying the Holocaust
work: The Historikerstreit (Historian's Quarrel)

front: Work: points to law as a mediating force between its two title concepts in a book about democracy and deliberative politics
work: General / Biographical and Other Works

front: Work: 1973 book introducing the concept that states face a crisis when they cannot achieve their goals with available means
work: General / Biographical and Other Works
